title: 11 lines of code took down the internet
hook: A dev got into a naming fight, rage-quit npm, and half the world's builds started failing. Here's exactly how it happened.

beats.heading: One dev, one naming fight
beats.html:
A startup wanted the npm name kik. A dev named Azer already had it. npm handed it to the company's lawyers, and Azer was not having it, so he unpublished all 273 of his packages.

Most? Nobody noticed. One of them? Oh, people noticed.

items.title: A user unpublished 273 of his modules this morning. We're seeing the fallout now.
An NPM package called left-pad broke half the build pipelines on earth

beats.caption: Eleven lines. Babel, React, webpack, and Node all leaned on this.

beats.title: zsh — building…
beats.caption: Red CI as far as the eye could see.

lines.t: npm ERR! 404 'left-pad' is not in the npm registry.

beats.heading: It wasn't just the people who used it
beats.html: It hit everyone who used something that used something that used left-pad. Transitive dependencies. Pull the brick out:

beats.text: I will not let a company take over a package I authored.
beats.cite: Azer Koçulu, on why he pulled everything

beats.heading: You don't actually own your dependencies
beats.html: Your app is a tower of strangers' code, and any brick can get yanked on a Tuesday. So: commit your lockfiles, vendor what you can't lose, and actually look at what npm install drags in.

sources.title: How one dev broke Node and the web
